label: Heavy going
summary: The world slips two points to 38, back into the 'Bad' band, as the Gulf war widens and Ukraine adds political turmoil to its military ordeal. The US completed a sixth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, with explosions reported in the port city of Bandar Abbas and on Qeshm Island and at least seven people killed, while Iran's army said it targeted American military sites in Jordan, Kuwait and Bahrain, drawing condemnation from the UAE and Saudi Arabia; neither side signals any willingness to negotiate. In Ukraine, Russian missiles struck Odesa on the evening of July 16, killing two people and injuring children, while President Zelensky's dismissal of popular defense minister Mykhailo Fedorov, architect of the drone program, brought more than a thousand protesters into Kyiv's central square chanting 'shame.' Israeli strikes killed at least five Palestinians in Gaza despite the nine-month-old ceasefire, under which 1,127 have now died. Africa's twin crises deepened: the WFP warned Sudan's hunger emergency is escalating as Hormuz disruption raises food-import costs and renewed Darfur fighting closed the Tine border crossing, and DR Congo's Ebola outbreak passed 2,000 confirmed cases with 754 deaths, with the WHO warning the true toll may be two to four times higher. Twin heat domes persisted, with a fresh surge pushing Iberia back into the mid-40s Celsius atop roughly 20,000 excess European deaths, and Salt Lake City logging an all-time 109F as wildfires burned in Colorado and Utah. Markets gave back some of Wednesday's record gains on a semiconductor selloff, though most early earnings beat estimates and Brent eased to around 84.6 dollars. The brighter column was real: four South American nations signed a 'Single Sky' open-skies pact, AI-guided researchers announced two new superconductors, the malaria-vaccine pioneer won the European Inventor Award, and the World Cup final between Argentina and Spain, Messi against Yamal, is set for Sunday. The headline matches the rubric formula with no adjustment.

drivers.headline: The US completes a sixth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, with at least three explosions heard in western Bandar Abbas, eight reported near Masan village on Qeshm Island in the Strait of Hormuz and the Kahurestan Bridge linking Bandar Abbas to Shiraz reportedly hit, killing at least seven people and wounding nine; Iran's army says it aimed attacks at American military targets in Jordan, Kuwait and Bahrain, drawing condemnation from the UAE and Saudi Arabia, and neither side publicly signals willingness to return to negotiations

Russian missiles strike Odesa on the evening of July 16, killing two people and injuring six including three children, damaging civilian infrastructure and the city's Professional College of Maritime and Railway Transport; the same night Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces hit 11 vessels of Russia's shadow fleet in the Black Sea and Sea of Azov, including five tankers, as the war's attritional exchange grinds on

President Zelensky fires Ukraine's popular defense minister Mykhailo Fedorov, the 35-year-old architect of the country's battlefield drone program, citing a rift with armed forces commander Oleksandr Syrskyi; more than a thousand protesters fill Kyiv's central square waving Ukrainian and EU flags and chanting 'shame,' with rallies in several cities expected to continue, injecting political instability into a country still under nightly attack

Israeli strikes kill at least five Palestinians across Gaza in a single day despite the October ceasefire, including two in an airstrike near the Tuffah neighbourhood and one in a strike on a tent sheltering displaced people west of Gaza City; the health ministry says 1,127 Palestinians have been killed and 3,643 injured since the truce took effect, and the Israeli military now controls well over half the territory

The World Food Programme warns Sudan's hunger crisis is escalating as war and Strait of Hormuz disruption drive up food-import costs: nearly 19.5 million people face acute food insecurity, famine risk looms over seven areas of North Darfur plus Dilling and Kadugli, renewed Darfur fighting has closed the Tine border crossing from Chad, and a 646-million-dollar funding gap has forced the agency to cut the number of people it assists from five million to about 3.5 million

DR Congo's Bundibugyo-virus Ebola outbreak tops 2,000 confirmed cases with 754 deaths, now the third-largest Ebola outbreak on record; 80 percent of new cases are not on known contact lists, the WHO warns the true scale may be two to four times the official tally, and a US aid worker who tested positive has been evacuated to Germany, though a clinical trial of the antibody MBP134 and the antiviral remdesivir is underway

Europe's deadly summer heat rolls on as a fresh heat dome reloads over the west of the continent, driving daytime highs 10 to 15C above normal and pushing Iberia and southern France back into the low-to-mid 40s Celsius; researchers now estimate roughly 20,390 excess deaths from the season's heatwaves, with Germany hardest hit at about 5,120, and national temperature records broken in more than a dozen countries

A second heat dome grips the western and central United States, with Salt Lake City recording an all-time high of 109F and Phoenix reaching 113F; the blistering heat is hindering crews fighting large wildfires in Colorado and Utah as extreme or exceptional drought covers much of the West and Great Plains, keeping fire risk above normal across the region

US stocks give back part of the week's record run as a semiconductor selloff drags the Nasdaq down 1.47 percent to 25,881.95 and the S&P 500 falls 0.51 percent to 7,533.77, with Alphabet sinking more than 4 percent on reports its Gemini 3.5 Pro model is behind schedule; the damage is cushioned by earnings strength, with more than 87 percent of early S&P 500 reporters beating estimates, and Brent crude easing to about 84.6 dollars a barrel

Aviation authorities from Argentina, Brazil, Chile and Paraguay sign a memorandum of understanding laying the groundwork for a South American Single Sky modelled on the EU's single aviation market, aiming to progressively remove national barriers to passenger and cargo traffic, add direct routes, boost competition and cut airfares across the continent

An international team led by Aalto University combines machine learning with quantum physics to discover two new superconductors, YRu3B2 and LuRu3B2, whose electrons form flat bands in a kagome lattice; the AI pre-screening approach dramatically speeds the search for new superconducting materials and marks a step toward the long-sought goal of room-temperature superconductivity

Anticipation builds for Sunday's World Cup final between defending champions Argentina and Spain at New York New Jersey Stadium, the first international showdown between Lionel Messi and Lamine Yamal, capping a record-crowd tournament; the week's positive ledger also includes Oxford malaria-vaccine pioneer Adrian Hill winning the European Inventor Award for the R21 shot now rolled out in some 20 nations, and Costa Rica's decades-long reversal of deforestation leaving more than half the country forested

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (2026 Iran war / 2026 European heatwaves / 2026 North American heat wave / 2026 United States wildfires)
